sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: SPACE, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 313, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- Goal model: Try SPACE at this position. If not, try moving eyes to other corners or positions.
- Plan: Try SPACE. If not done, try RIGHT to bring eyes together, or UP to move eyes up.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 312 actions; a strong score needs about 30 or fewer. You are 10.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK, so after pressing SPACE at (49, 9) and (49, 49), the board changed but the level wasn't completed. Then I pressed RIGHT 3 times:
- RIGHT 1: (49, 14) and (49, 44)
- RIGHT 2: (49, 19) and (49, 39)
- RIGHT 3: (49, 24) and (49, 34)
- RIGHT 4: No board change (stuck)
So the eyes moved from (49, 9) and (49, 49) to (49, 14) and (49, 44), then to (49, 19) and (49, 39), then to (49, 24) and (49, 34).
The eyes are now at (49, 24) and (49, 34). Both eyes are at row 49, but at different columns (24 and 34).
This is the minimum horizontal separation I've seen so far when the eyes are at row 49. The eyes are close together, but not overlapping.
But pressing SPACE at this position didn't complete the level (as I observed earlier... wait, actually I didn't press SPACE at this position in this run).
Let me try pressing SPACE at (49, 24) and (49, 34).
Actually, wait. I notice that SPACE at (49, 9) and (49, 49) resulted in board_changed=True. This means the board changed when I pressed SPACE. But the level wasn't completed. So what changed?
Let me check the current frame to see what changed.
Looking at the current image, the eyes are at (49, 24) and (49, 34). Both eyes are at row 49, which is the bottom row of the black shape. The left eye is at col 24, and the right eye is at col 34.
But wait, the initial position was (44, 19) and (44, 39). So the eyes are now at row 49, which is 5 rows below the initial row 44.
Hmm, I notice that the eyes moved down when I pressed LEFT. From (44, 19) and (44, 39), pressing LEFT moved the eyes to (49, 14) and (49, 44). So the eyes moved down 5 rows and left/right 5 columns.
This is interesting. The eyes' movement is not just horizontal; it's also vertical. When I press LEFT, the left eye moves left and down, and the right eye moves right and down.
Wait, but earlier I observed that when I pressed LEFT from (9, 44) and (39, 44), the left eye moved left to col 39 (and stayed at row 9), and the right eye moved right to col 49 (and stayed at row 39). So the eyes didn't move vertically.
So the eyes' vertical movement depends on their current position. At (44, 19) and (44, 39), pressing LEFT moves the eyes down. But at (9, 44) and (39, 44), pressing LEFT doesn't move the eyes vertically.
This is confusing. Let me think about this more carefully.
Actually, I think the eyes' movement is constrained by the black shape. The black shape has a certain outline, and the eyes can only move within this outline. When I press LEFT, the eyes move left and right (spreading apart), but they also move vertically to stay within the black shape.
At (44, 19) and (44, 39), the eyes are near the bottom of the black shape. When I press LEFT, the eyes move left and right, but they also move down to stay within the black shape. So the left eye moves from (44, 19) to (49, 14), and the right eye moves from (44, 39) to (49, 44).
At (9, 44) and (39, 44), the eyes are near the top of the black shape. When I press LEFT, the eyes move left and right, but they stay at the same row because the black shape extends horizontally at that row. So the left eye moves from (9, 44) to (9, 39), and the right eye moves from (39, 44) to (39, 49).
OK, so the eyes' movement is constrained by the black shape's outline. The eyes move in the direction of the input, but they also adjust vertically to stay within the black shape.
This makes sense. The black shape is a face-like shape, and the eyes move within this shape.
Now, back to the goal. I've tried pressing SPACE at many positions, but none of them completed the level. What if the goal is not about pressing SPACE at a specific position, but about something else?
What if the goal is to move the eyes to a position where they're at the same column? Like, both eyes at col 32?
Currently, the eyes are at (49, 24) and (49, 34). The left eye is at col 24, and the right eye is at col 34. They're 10 columns apart.
If I press RIGHT, the left eye moves right and the right eye moves left (coming together). But pressing RIGHT from (49, 24) and (49, 34) resulted in no board change, so the eyes are stuck at this position.
So the eyes can't move closer together than 10 columns apart at row 49.
What if I press UP to move the eyes up, and then press RIGHT to bring them closer together?
Let me try pressing UP from (49, 24) and (49, 34).

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model (REVISED):
- Eyes at (49, 24) and (49, 34) - same row, close together
- SPACE at (49, 9) and (49, 49) changed board but didn't complete level
- Eyes stuck at (49, 24) and (49, 34) - can't move RIGHT further
- Need to try UP to move eyes up, then try different configurations
Goal model: Maybe need eyes at same column? Or need specific eye configuration + SPACE?
Plan: Try UP to move eyes up. Then try RIGHT to bring eyes closer. Try SPACE at new positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current: eyes at (49, 24) and (49, 34)
# Try UP to move eyes up
for i in range(10):
    result = action(['UP'])
    seg = current_frame.segmentation
    sb_nodes = [n for n in seg['nodes'] if n['color'] == 'S']
    positions = sorted([(min(p[0] for p in n['boundary']), min(p[1] for p in n['boundary'])) for n in sb_nodes])
    print(f"UP {i+1}: {positions}")
    if result.get('done'):
        print(f"  DONE!")
        break
    if not result.get('board_changed', True):
        print(f"  No board change, stopping")
        break
    # Try SPACE at each position
    result2 = action(['SPACE'])
    print(f"  SPACE: done={result2.get('done')}")
    if result2.get('done'):
        break
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.content:

  SPACE: done=False

  SPACE: done=False
UP 3: [(44, 24), (44, 34)]
  SPACE: done=False
UP 4: [(44, 24), (44, 34)]
  No board change, stopping
